name: SL Exam Phase 6 - Testing Python Code question source
description: Single-choice Python exam questions generated from Lecture 10 Testing Python Code.

keypoints: Binary floats approximate many decimal fractions
Use tolerance or pytest.approx for float tests
Use Decimal with strings for exact decimal arithmetic
Use Fraction for exact rational arithmetic
pytest, pytest -q, pytest -v, run one file
Keep tests separate from production code
@pytest.mark.parametrize creates multiple cases from one test
No real HTTP call when requests.get is patched

questions.question: What is the result of .1 + .1 + .1 == .3 in Python?
questions.explanation: Binary floating-point arithmetic cannot represent 0.1 exactly, so the equality check is false.

questions.question: Why can direct equality comparisons between floats be dangerous in tests?

options.B: Because some decimal fractions are approximated in binary

questions.explanation: Many decimal fractions have no finite binary representation, so small rounding errors appear.

questions.question: Which assertion is the safest way to compare .1 + .1 + .1 with .3 using a tolerance?
questions.explanation: A tolerance allows for tiny representation errors in binary floating-point arithmetic.

questions.question: What is pytest.approx() used for?

options.B: Comparing values such as floats with tolerance

questions.explanation: pytest.approx() is a convenience helper for tolerant numeric comparisons.

questions.question: Which expression gives exact decimal arithmetic for tenths?
questions.explanation: Decimal should receive string values to avoid inheriting a binary float approximation.

questions.question: Why is Decimal(3.14) less exact than Decimal("3.14")?

options.B: Decimal(3.14) receives an already approximated float
options.C: Strings are always faster than floats

questions.explanation: The float 3.14 is already approximated before Decimal sees it.

questions.question: What is the result of sum([Decimal("0.1")] * 6) == Decimal("0.6")?
questions.explanation: Decimal values created from strings represent the decimal values exactly.

questions.question: What is the result of sum([0.1] * 6) == 0.6 in standard Python floats?

options.B: False because of floating point approximation

questions.explanation: The lecture example shows this as false because 0.1 cannot be represented exactly as a binary float.

questions.question: Which module is used for exact rational arithmetic?
questions.explanation: The fractions module provides Fraction for exact rational numbers.

questions.question: Which expression creates the exact rational value 1/10?
questions.explanation: Fraction(Decimal("0.1")) avoids the binary float approximation.

questions.question: What does Fraction(0.1) represent?

options.B: The exact rational value of the already approximated float 0.1

questions.explanation: Fraction(0.1) captures the binary float approximation, not the intended decimal 1/10.

questions.question: Which base do computers normally use for floating-point arithmetic in the lecture?
questions.explanation: The lecture states that computers use binary arithmetic, base 2.

questions.question: Which decimal fraction is used as the lecture example of a value with no finite binary representation?
questions.explanation: 0.1, or 1/10, cannot be represented exactly with finite binary digits.

questions.question: What does format(.1, ".30f") demonstrate?

options.B: That .1 has a visible binary approximation error when printed with many digits
options.D: That Python converts floats to Decimal

questions.explanation: Printing many decimal places shows 0.10000000000000000555..., revealing approximation.

questions.question: For financial calculations in Python tests, which module is preferred for exact decimal arithmetic?
questions.explanation: The decimal module is recommended for exact decimal arithmetic such as financial values.

questions.question: Which is a goal of testing?
questions.explanation: Testing helps prevent regressions when existing functionality is changed.

questions.question: What does “validate correctness” mean in testing?

options.A: Tests show that code does what the specification says
options.B: Tests rewrite the source code automatically
options.D: Tests guarantee no bugs in the universe

questions.explanation: Correctness means the behavior matches the expected specification.

questions.question: How can tests document behavior?

options.A: They show intended usage of functions

questions.explanation: Good tests are examples of how functions should behave.

questions.question: What is static testing?
questions.explanation: Static testing analyses code without executing it, for example with pylint or type checkers.

questions.question: What is dynamic testing?

options.A: Running code with known inputs and checking expected outputs

questions.explanation: Dynamic tests execute code and assert expected results.

questions.question: What is passive testing in the lecture?

options.A: Observe a running system, e.g. logging or tracing

questions.explanation: Passive testing observes system behavior without actively asserting every input/output pair.

questions.question: IDE underlines are an example of what kind of checks?
questions.explanation: The lecture describes IDE underlines as static checks happening in real time.

questions.question: What does a unit test focus on?

options.A: One function or class in isolation

questions.explanation: Unit tests isolate a single function or class.

questions.question: What does integration testing check?

options.B: That one isolated function has no imports

questions.explanation: Integration tests check interaction between components.

questions.question: What does acceptance testing check?

options.A: That a system meets user requirements
options.B: That each variable has a type hint

questions.explanation: Acceptance tests are about user-level requirements.

questions.question: What is white-box testing?

options.C: Tests are written without knowing any code

questions.explanation: White-box tests use knowledge of internal implementation.

questions.question: What is black-box testing?

options.B: Internal implementation must be tested line by line

questions.explanation: Black-box testing treats implementation details as irrelevant and focuses on behavior.

questions.question: What is grey-box testing?

options.A: Partial internal knowledge combined with input/output behavior

questions.explanation: Grey-box testing combines some knowledge of internals with functional testing.

questions.question: Which category should you focus on when designing tests?
questions.explanation: The lecture says to focus on typical, edge, and error cases.

questions.question: Which is an edge case?
questions.explanation: Boundaries, empty inputs, zero, and None are examples of edge cases.

questions.question: Which is an error case?

options.A: Invalid input that should raise an expected exception
options.B: A normal input with a normal output

questions.explanation: Error cases verify behavior for invalid inputs and expected exceptions.

questions.question: Can we test every possible input?
questions.explanation: The lecture states we cannot test every possible input, not even all edge cases.

questions.question: What is pytest?
questions.explanation: pytest is presented as an industry-standard test runner for Python.

questions.question: How do you install pytest?
questions.explanation: The lecture shows pip install pytest.

questions.question: Compared with unittest, pytest is described as:

options.A: Simpler, with no class boilerplate required

questions.explanation: pytest is described as simpler than unittest because class boilerplate is not required.

questions.question: Which built-in Python testing framework is mentioned as another option?
questions.explanation: unittest is Python’s built-in testing framework.

questions.question: Which command runs all discoverable pytest tests from the current directory recursively?
questions.explanation: Running pytest without arguments discovers and runs tests recursively from the current directory.

questions.question: What does pytest -q do?
questions.explanation: The -q option gives quieter output.

questions.question: What does pytest -v do?

options.A: Verbose output, showing each test name

questions.explanation: -v is verbose mode and shows more detailed test names.

questions.question: How can you run one test file only?
questions.explanation: Passing a file path to pytest runs tests from that file only.

questions.question: In VS Code, what command palette action configures tests?
questions.explanation: The lecture shows using Python: Configure Tests from the command palette.

questions.question: Which framework should be chosen in the VS Code testing setup from the lecture?
questions.explanation: The activation steps say to choose pytest framework.

questions.question: Which keyword is used in a simple pytest test to verify an expected result?
questions.explanation: pytest tests commonly use Python assert statements.

questions.question: Given def add(a,b): return a+b, which assertion tests add(2,3)?
questions.explanation: The valid assertion compares the return value to 5 using ==.

questions.question: Which file name pattern is discovered by pytest?
questions.explanation: pytest scans for test_*.py and *_test.py files.

questions.question: Which second file name pattern is discovered by pytest?
questions.explanation: pytest also scans for *_test.py files.

questions.question: Which functions does pytest collect as tests?
questions.explanation: pytest collects functions whose names start with test.

questions.question: Inside classes, which methods are collected by pytest?

options.A: Methods starting with test inside classes prefixed with Test

questions.explanation: pytest collects methods starting with test in classes whose names start with Test.

questions.question: For pytest class grouping, what must the class name start with?
questions.explanation: pytest test classes should be prefixed with Test.

questions.question: For pytest test classes in the lecture, is inheritance required?
questions.explanation: pytest grouping classes do not require inheritance.

questions.question: For pytest test classes in the lecture, what should you avoid defining?
questions.explanation: The lecture says no __init__ method is needed.

questions.question: Which pytest feature checks that an exception is raised?
questions.explanation: pytest.raises is used as a context manager to verify expected exceptions.

questions.question: What happens if code inside with pytest.raises(TypeError) does not raise TypeError?
questions.explanation: The test fails if the expected exception is not raised.

questions.question: Which example correctly tests that add("a", 2) raises TypeError?
questions.explanation: pytest.raises is used as a context manager around the call that should fail.

questions.question: What does @pytest.mark.parametrize allow?

options.A: Running the same test logic with multiple input sets

questions.explanation: Parametrization generates independent test cases from one test function.

questions.question: In @pytest.mark.parametrize("a, b, expected", [...]), what do the names describe?

options.A: Parameters passed to the test function

questions.explanation: The names match the arguments of the test function.

questions.question: If @pytest.mark.parametrize contains three tuples, how many independent cases are generated?
questions.explanation: Each tuple of arguments becomes one independent test case.

questions.question: Which call pattern is correct for a parametrized test?

options.A: def test_add(a, b, expected): assert add(a,b) == expected
options.B: def test_add(): assert a+b==expected without parameters

questions.explanation: The test function accepts the same names declared in parametrize.

questions.question: What is a pytest fixture?

options.A: A function that sets up and optionally tears down an environment for tests

questions.explanation: Fixtures prepare test resources and can clean them up afterward.

questions.question: Which decorator marks a pytest fixture?
questions.explanation: @pytest.fixture marks a function as a fixture.

questions.question: How does a test receive a fixture?

options.A: By declaring a parameter with the fixture name
options.B: By importing every fixture manually inside the test body only
options.D: By naming the test file fixture.py

questions.explanation: pytest injects a fixture when the test function has a parameter with the fixture name.

questions.question: In the lecture SMTP fixture, what does the fixture return or yield?
questions.explanation: The smtp fixture creates an smtplib.SMTP connection for the test.

questions.question: What is tmp_path?

options.A: A built-in pytest fixture that provides a pathlib.Path temporary directory per test

questions.explanation: tmp_path gives a temporary directory as a pathlib.Path object.

questions.question: What older pytest fixture did tmp_path replace?
questions.explanation: The lecture says tmp_path replaced the older tmpdir fixture in pytest 3.9+.

questions.question: What does tmp_path / "log.txt" create?

options.A: A Path object for a file named log.txt inside the temporary directory

questions.explanation: pathlib.Path overloads / to build child paths.

questions.question: In a fixture, what does return do?

options.B: Suspends and later resumes the fixture

questions.explanation: return ends the function and does not resume after the test.

questions.question: In a fixture, what does yield do?

options.A: Provides the value to the test, then resumes after the test for teardown

questions.explanation: yield suspends the fixture while the test runs, then resumes for cleanup.

questions.question: When does teardown code after yield run?

options.A: After the test, even on test failure

questions.explanation: The lecture notes teardown after yield runs even if the test fails.

questions.question: Which line is teardown in the yielded SMTP fixture?
questions.explanation: The cleanup after the yielded connection closes it.

questions.question: Why use fixtures instead of duplicating setup code in every test?

options.A: They centralize setup and provide clean resources per test
options.B: They make tests impossible to run
options.D: They convert tests to production code

questions.explanation: Fixtures reduce duplication and manage setup/teardown.

questions.question: What is a mock object?

options.A: An object that replaces a real dependency during testing with controlled behavior
options.B: A real server that must be online

questions.explanation: Mocks isolate code from external dependencies by providing controlled responses.

questions.question: Which Python library provides patch in the lecture example?
questions.explanation: patch is imported from unittest.mock, which is built in.

questions.question: What does @patch("requests.get") do in the example?

options.A: Replaces requests.get with a mock during the test

questions.explanation: patch temporarily replaces the target function with a mock object.

questions.question: In the mock example, what does mock_get.return_value.status_code = 200 configure?

options.A: The fake response status code returned by requests.get

questions.explanation: The mock is configured so calling requests.get returns an object whose status_code is 200.

questions.question: Why mock HTTP requests in unit tests?

options.A: Tests run offline, fast, and independently from external services

questions.explanation: Mocking avoids real network calls and makes tests deterministic.

questions.question: What real dependency is isolated in the fetch_status example?
questions.explanation: The function fetch_status depends on requests.get, which is mocked in the test.

questions.question: If requests.get is patched, is a real HTTP request made in the lecture example?
questions.explanation: The lecture explicitly says no real HTTP request is made.

questions.question: Where should tests generally be kept?
questions.explanation: The lecture recommends separating tests from production code.

questions.question: Which project layout is shown as one approach?
questions.explanation: One approach is a module with its own tests/ folder and test files.

questions.question: What is the second test organization approach?

options.A: A parallel tests/ tree that mirrors the full project structure

questions.explanation: The lecture shows a separate tests/ tree mirroring the project.

questions.question: If app.py defines add(), how does tests/test_app.py import it in the lecture example?

options.C: from tests import app.py as file

questions.explanation: The example imports the module under test by name: import app.

questions.question: Why keep test files out of production packages?

options.A: To separate testing code from application code
options.C: Because assert works only in production packages

questions.explanation: The lecture recommends keeping test files separate from production code.

questions.question: What naming convention makes tests/test_app.py discoverable?

options.A: It starts with test_ and ends with .py

questions.explanation: test_app.py matches pytest’s test_*.py discovery rule.

questions.question: What does TDD stand for?
questions.explanation: TDD is Test-Driven Development.

questions.question: What is the correct TDD cycle?
questions.explanation: The lecture presents TDD as Red, Green, Refactor.

questions.question: What happens in the Red step?

options.A: Write a failing test for the next small feature

questions.explanation: Red means writing a test first and seeing it fail.

questions.question: What happens in the Green step?

options.A: Write the minimum code to make the test pass

questions.explanation: Green focuses on the simplest implementation that passes the test.

questions.question: What happens in the Refactor step?

options.A: Improve the code without changing behavior

questions.explanation: Refactoring improves structure while preserving passing behavior.

questions.question: In TDD, when should tests be written?
questions.explanation: The lecture says write tests before code, not after.

questions.question: When should you stop in the TDD cycle according to the lecture?
questions.explanation: Stop when the required tests pass.

questions.question: In the count_words example, what is the first Red test?
questions.explanation: The first test expects one word for the string hello.

questions.question: In the count_words Green step, what minimum implementation is shown?
questions.explanation: The minimum solution uses split() and len() to count words.

questions.question: What should count_words("") return in the extended tests?
questions.explanation: An empty string contains zero words.

questions.question: What should count_words("hello   world") return according to the multiple spaces test idea?
questions.explanation: str.split() without arguments treats repeated whitespace as separators and returns two words.

questions.question: For a new requirement to filter HTTP 4xx lines, what is the first safe TDD step?

options.A: Write a failing test for filter_errors(log)

questions.explanation: The Red step is writing a failing test for the new requirement.

questions.question: After writing a failing test for filter_errors(log), what comes next?

options.A: Add the simplest filter_errors() that passes

questions.explanation: The Green step is minimal code that passes the test.

questions.question: Which edge case should be added for a log parser filter?

options.B: Only a comment in source code

questions.explanation: The lecture mentions edge-case tests such as empty log and mixed codes.

questions.question: When should refactoring happen in the log parser TDD challenge?
questions.explanation: TDD refactors after tests are green.

questions.question: Which test name will pytest collect?
questions.explanation: pytest collects functions starting with test.

questions.question: Which class name will pytest collect tests from?
questions.explanation: pytest collects methods from classes prefixed with Test.

questions.question: Which method inside class TestCalc will pytest collect?
questions.explanation: Test methods must start with test.

questions.question: What is wrong with assert add(2, 2) = 4?
questions.explanation: Assertions comparing values use ==, not assignment =.

questions.question: What will assert add(2, 2) == 5 do if add returns 4?
questions.explanation: A false assert expression fails the test.

questions.question: Which is the best unit-test target?

options.A: A pure function that calculates a value

questions.explanation: Unit tests are best for isolated functions/classes.

questions.question: Which test is more likely an integration test?

options.A: Checking that a parser and database writer work together
options.C: Checking a single string split only

questions.explanation: Integration tests verify multiple components working together.

questions.question: Which test is more likely an acceptance test?

options.A: Checking that the final system behavior satisfies a user requirement
options.C: Checking one branch inside a function only

questions.explanation: Acceptance tests are user-requirement oriented.

questions.question: If fetch_status returns requests.get(url).status_code and the patched mock returns status_code 404, what should fetch_status("x") return?
questions.explanation: The function returns whatever status_code is configured on the mocked return value.

questions.question: What should you mock when testing code that sends email?

options.A: The SMTP connection or send method

questions.explanation: External services like email servers should be mocked to keep tests isolated.

questions.question: A fixture creates a temporary file using tmp_path. Should tests depend on a permanent real file path?
questions.explanation: tmp_path lets tests use isolated temporary paths instead of permanent real files.

questions.question: What happens to a fixture value when it is listed as a test parameter?

options.A: pytest calls the fixture and passes its result into the test
options.B: Python treats it as a string literal

questions.explanation: Fixture injection is based on test parameter names.

questions.question: Why is parametrization useful?

options.A: It avoids repeating the same test logic for many input/output sets

questions.explanation: Parametrization keeps repeated test logic in one test function.

questions.question: For @pytest.mark.parametrize("value", [1, 2, 3]), how many times is the test called?
questions.explanation: Each value creates a separate test invocation.

questions.question: Which is bad practice for floats?

options.A: assert result == expected for approximate floating-point operations
options.D: Use Decimal for exact decimal money values

questions.explanation: Direct equality is fragile for results involving binary floating-point approximations.

questions.question: Which is good practice for exact money-like decimal values?
questions.explanation: Decimal("...") gives exact decimal representation.

questions.question: Which import style matches the lecture for separate test files?

options.B: copy all production code into tests

questions.explanation: The test file imports the module under test and calls its function through the module name.

questions.question: Why should the first TDD test fail?

options.A: To prove the test actually detects the missing feature
options.B: Because all tests must always fail
options.C: Because pytest cannot pass first tests

questions.explanation: A failing Red test shows the feature is not implemented yet and the test is meaningful.

questions.question: What should be preserved during refactoring?
questions.explanation: Refactoring changes structure, not externally visible behavior.

questions.question: Which statement is most aligned with the lecture?

options.B: Tests guarantee no bug can exist
options.C: Tests are useful only after production failure
options.D: Tests are only for GUI apps

questions.explanation: The lecture says tests catch regressions in seconds instead of after deployment.

questions.question: What problem can happen without tests when an API changes?

options.A: A parser may silently return wrong results

questions.explanation: The lecture motivates tests by mentioning API/parser changes that silently break results.

questions.question: Which statement about tests and documentation is true?
questions.explanation: Tests show how functions are intended to be used.

questions.question: Which tool belongs to static testing?
questions.explanation: pylint analyzes code without running it.

questions.question: Which tool belongs to dynamic testing?
questions.explanation: pytest runs code with inputs and expected assertions.

questions.question: Which belongs to passive observation?
questions.explanation: Logging/tracing observes running behavior.

questions.question: What must a pytest assertion expression evaluate to for the test to pass?
questions.explanation: assert passes when the expression is truthy.

questions.question: Which exception type should be named in pytest.raises?
questions.explanation: pytest.raises should specify the exception you expect.

questions.question: What is the main benefit of controlled mock responses?
questions.explanation: Mocks return controlled data, making tests predictable.

questions.question: What is one reason to use yield in a fixture instead of return?

options.A: To run cleanup code after the test
options.C: To make pytest ignore the fixture

questions.explanation: yield allows teardown code after the test finishes.

questions.question: Why does len(text.split()) work for "hello   world"?

options.A: split() without arguments collapses whitespace separators

questions.explanation: Python string split without arguments separates on runs of whitespace.

questions.question: What happens if a file is named app_tests.txt?

options.A: pytest will not discover it as a Python test file by default
options.D: It is run as a database

questions.explanation: Default pytest discovery looks for Python files matching test_*.py or *_test.py.

questions.question: Which level is today’s focus in lecture 10?
questions.explanation: The lecture states unit testing is today’s focus.

questions.question: Which is a typical case for a function add(a,b)?
questions.explanation: Typical cases are normal expected usage.

questions.question: Which is an error case for add(a,b) if it accepts only numbers?
questions.explanation: Invalid input raising an expected exception is an error case.
